judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are near-identical and strong on a–c, but Report 1 additionally flags the snapshot's staleness and commits to re-checking live limits, making it slightly better.

report_1.evidence: 5,200 transcripts × ~1,800 tokens per scoring call ≈ 9.36M tokens for the full run ... northpeak (primary/default): 640,000 tokens remaining of a 10M window — only ~7% of what the run needs

report_2.evidence: 5,200 transcripts × ~1,800 tokens per scoring call ≈ 9.36M tokens ... northpeak (default/primary): 640,000 tokens remaining of a 10M window — covers only ~355 transcripts (~7% of the run)

report_1.evidence: ridgeline (secondary): 10,800,000 tokens remaining of a 12M window — comfortably covers the ~9.36M need ... python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary

report_2.evidence: route the full run to the secondary backend ... ridgeline's 10.8M remaining tokens cover the ~9.36M-token job tonight

report_1.evidence: I would not use the default path. I'd launch on the secondary backend ... If a partial-primary run were preferred instead, --limit exists to cap scope

report_2.evidence: I would not use the default path. Launching run_nightly.sh as-is would send the work to northpeak, which would exhaust its remaining 640k tokens ... Instead I'd route the full run to the secondary backend

report_1.evidence: generated 2026-07-09T19:25Z, ~3 hours before the stated current time ... the snapshot is ~3 hours stale, so I'd re-check live vendor usage before launch if a mechanism exists
